callouts.content: SKILL: /ccwork — Onboarding hub and lab runner. Loads lab exercises, creates tracking issues, and sets up starting branches.

SKILL: /name — Report or pick the agent's session identity (Dev-Name, Dev-Avatar, Dev-Team). The identity is stored in a JSON file keyed by the MD5 hash of the project root, so it's project-scoped but session-ephemeral.
SKILL: /engage — Session onboarding ritual. Reads CLAUDE.md, confirms rules of engagement, and loads any existing plans. Always run this at session start.
SKILL: /view — Opens a file in the system's default GUI viewer for visual inspection outside the terminal.
SKILL: /precheck — Pre-commit quality gate. Verifies branch/issue linkage, runs tests and lint, executes code review, and presents the mandatory checklist.

The two-layer identity model: Dev-Team is project-level (persists in CLAUDE.md across sessions), while Dev-Name and Dev-Avatar are session-level (stored in /tmp, keyed by MD5 hash of the project root). This means each new session gets a fresh persona, but the team affiliation is stable.

The test only checks that the placeholder text is gone — it doesn't assert specific replacement text. This is negative-assertion test design: validate that work was done without constraining how. It gives the agent creative freedom while still verifying the lab objective.

Watch the design conversation: layout strategy (tiled cards with terminal-width awareness), library choice (Rich vs hand-rolled), and color palette (cyberpunk theme) — all decided before a single line of code. This front-loading of design decisions prevents rework.

The user catches the agent about to start coding without an issue. 'Before we build it we need to /ibm.' This is the Issue → Branch → MR discipline in action — even when excitement about a feature runs high, the workflow comes first. The agent had the right instinct but wrong sequence.

TERM=dumb is the trick for testing Rich output in CI/headless environments. Rich auto-detects terminal capabilities and adjusts output — setting TERM=dumb forces plain text mode, making test assertions predictable regardless of where they run.

The user challenges the 'tested' checkbox: 'Did we test it?' The agent listed actual execution runs as evidence. Claiming 'tested' means you ran it — not that it should work, not that the linter passed. This is the difference between the checklist being a rubber stamp vs. a real gate.

Even though /scpmmr is a single command, it didn't skip any safety gates — precheck was verified, approval was obtained, each step was confirmed. Automation doesn't mean bypassing quality checks; it means executing them consistently.

artifacts.title: Cyberpunk Dashboard Color Palette
artifacts.description: The color scheme designed for the identity dashboard
artifacts.content:
| Role | Color | Hex | Usage |
|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Primary | Matrix Green | `#00ff41` | Borders, structure |
| Secondary | Deep Fuchsia | `#e100ff` | Values, emphasis |
| Accent | Cyan | `#00ffff` | Metadata, accents |
| Base | Dim White | — | Body text |
